questions.question: Which statement best describes the role of couplant in contact testing?
options: It reduces the operating frequency of the transducer
It is part of the sound path, filling the air gap so energy can cross from the probe into the part
It is only needed on vertical surfaces
It amplifies the returning echo electronically
questions.explanation: Couplant displaces the trapped air at the interface so the sound can cross into the part; without it the steel-to-air boundary reflects nearly all the energy. It is acoustically part of the path, not a lubricant or amplifier.

questions.question: A technician must inspect a weld on an in-service pipe forty feet up on a rack, working from one side with portable gear. The most appropriate basic method is:
questions.explanation: Contact testing is portable, single-sided, and fast to set up, which suits field weld inspection. Immersion and air-coupled methods need tanks or two-sided access not available on an elevated in-service pipe.

questions.question: Scanning a curved pipe surface with a flat-faced contact probe tends to cause the probe to rock, which primarily results in:
options: Lost contact at the edges, varying coupling and a shifted refracted angle
questions.explanation: On a curved surface a flat probe loses contact at the edges, so coupling varies and the beam angle changes — corrupting both amplitude and flaw location. A contoured wedge or axial orientation fixes it.

questions.question: In immersion testing the constant water path is valuable mainly because it:
options: Removes the need to calibrate the instrument
Keeps coupling identical at every scan position, making amplitudes repeatable for automated acceptance
Allows inspection without knowing the sound velocity
questions.explanation: Because the probe never touches the part, the water path keeps coupling constant everywhere, so amplitude changes reflect real material changes — the basis of mechanized C-scan acceptance.

questions.question: A 12.5 mm thick steel part is examined by flat immersion. Using the 4:1 steel-to-water velocity ratio, the minimum water path before adding a margin is about:
questions.explanation: Water path ≥ T × (v_water/v_steel) = 12.5 ÷ 4 ≈ 3 mm. A margin of roughly 6 mm is then added so the back-wall echo clears the second front-surface multiple. (50 mm — multiplying by 4 — is the inverted rule.)

questions.question: A focused immersion probe is set so its focal point lies in the water. When the beam enters the steel, the focal depth in the metal will be:
options: Shorter (shallower), because refraction shortens the focal length by roughly the velocity ratio
Eliminated, because steel cannot be focused
questions.explanation: At the water-to-steel interface the beam refracts and the focal length shortens by about the velocity ratio, so the focus lands much shallower in the metal. The standoff is chosen to place the focus at the depth of interest.

questions.question: Which display would an immersion system produce to show a cross-sectional view of reflectors through the thickness along a scan line?
questions.explanation: A B-scan is the cross-sectional (side-view) image through the thickness; a C-scan is the top-down plan view, and an A-scan is a single-point waveform.

questions.question: Air-coupled ultrasonic testing typically operates at frequencies of:
options: Tens of kilohertz up to about 1 MHz
Exactly the same frequencies as contact testing
questions.explanation: Low frequencies cross the air-to-solid mismatch and travel through air with less loss, so air-coupled work runs well below 1 MHz, often in the tens to hundreds of kilohertz.

questions.question: A consequence of using through-transmission in air-coupled testing is that the method:
options: Provides no depth information and requires access to both sides of the part
Needs only one transducer on one side
Works best on thick steel forgings
questions.explanation: Through-transmission measures transmitted energy rather than a timed echo, so it cannot report depth and needs a transmitter and receiver aligned on opposite faces. A flaw appears as a drop in transmitted signal.

questions.question: Across the three basic methods, the fundamental problem each one solves differently is:
options: How to calibrate the time base
How to get sound across the impedance gap between the probe and the part
How to set the pulse repetition frequency
questions.explanation: Contact bridges the gap with gel, immersion with a water path, and air coupling engineers around the loss with low frequency and through-transmission — all answers to crossing the probe-to-part impedance gap.
